label: Information Bottleneck
definition: A principle that frames learning as compressing the input into a representation that discards everything irrelevant while keeping the information needed to predict the target.

body.text: The information bottleneck is an information-theoretic account of what a good representation is. Formulated by Tishby, Pereira, and Bialek in 1999, it asks a learner to find an internal representation of the input that is maximally informative about the output label while being maximally compressed about the input itself. These two goals pull in opposite directions, so the principle is stated as a tradeoff: minimize the mutual-information between the input and the representation, subject to keeping the mutual information between the representation and the target high. A single parameter sets the exchange rate between compression and prediction.

The appeal is that this gives a definition of "relevant" information without reference to any particular architecture. The ideal representation is a minimal sufficient statistic of the input for the task: it throws away every detail of the input that does not help predict the label, and keeps every detail that does. Nuisance variation, the parts of the input uncorrelated with the answer, gets squeezed out at the bottleneck, which is exactly the kind of invariance we want a model to learn. This connects the information bottleneck to entropy, kl-divergence, and the broader project of describing learning in the currency of bits.

The principle drew wide attention when Tishby and Shwartz-Ziv proposed in 2017 that deep networks trained by gradient descent pass through two phases visible in the information plane: a short fitting phase where the layers gain information about the label, followed by a long compression phase where they discard information about the input. In this telling, generalization comes from compression, and the layered structure of a deep network implements a chain of successive bottlenecks. The claim was influential and also contested, since the measured dynamics depend on how mutual information is estimated and on the choice of activation function, and the debate sharpened the field's care about information-theoretic measurement in high dimensions.

Beyond the explanatory story, the information bottleneck is a usable training objective. Deep variational versions, closely related to the variational-autoencoder, optimize a tractable bound on the bottleneck tradeoff and have been used to build representations that are robust, compact, and less prone to memorizing spurious detail. The framework also clarifies failure modes: collapse the bottleneck too hard and the representation keeps too little to predict well, a form of representation-collapse, while too little compression leaves nuisance information that hurts generalization. The principle thus offers one unified axis, bits kept versus bits discarded, along which to reason about both representation quality and the bias toward simple solutions captured by inductive-bias.

image.alt: A schematic of the information plane. The horizontal axis is I(z;x), the mutual information between the representation and the input, and the vertical axis is I(z;y), the mutual information between the representation and the target. A trajectory of red dots first moves right and up (the fitting phase, I(z;x) increases) and then curves back left while staying high (the compression phase, I(z;x) decreases).
image.caption: The information bottleneck is stated in exactly the two quantities this plane tracks: how much a representation tells you about the input, I(z;x), against how much it tells you about the target, I(z;y). A good representation pushes up I(z;y) while squeezing down I(z;x), the compression phase shown here, keeping what predicts the label and discarding the rest of the input.
